---
title: "Mathematical and Search Substrates"
chapter_id: "mathematical-and-search-substrates"
part_id: "routing-compression-representation-substrates"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "genesiscode"
  - "temporal_coil_research"
  - "cognitive_compilation"
  - "treellm"
  - "simulation_scaling"
  - "coilmoecot"
  - "circle_calculus_core"
  - "circle_ai_architectures"
  - "proof_carrying_circular_computation"
  - "theseus_circle_transfer"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Mathematical and Search Substrates

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `mathematical-and-search-substrates` |
| Part | Part III - Routing, Compression, Representation, and Substrates |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `genesiscode`, `temporal_coil_research`, `cognitive_compilation`, `treellm`, `simulation_scaling`, `coilmoecot`, `circle_calculus_core`, `circle_ai_architectures`, `proof_carrying_circular_computation`, `theseus_circle_transfer` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `genesiscode`, `temporal_coil_research`; supporting: `cognitive_compilation`, `treellm`, `simulation_scaling`, `circle_calculus_core`, `circle_ai_architectures`, `proof_carrying_circular_computation`, `theseus_circle_transfer`; connector/recovery: `coilmoecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `coilmoecot`; raw cache: `genesiscode`, `temporal_coil_research`, `cognitive_compilation`, `treellm`, `simulation_scaling`, `coilmoecot`; pending source notes: `genesiscode`, `temporal_coil_research`, `cognitive_compilation`, `treellm`, `simulation_scaling`, `circle_calculus_core`, `circle_ai_architectures`, `proof_carrying_circular_computation`, `theseus_circle_transfer` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

The source corpus contains exploratory mathematical/search substrates that may matter but must not be overclaimed.

Within the ASI Stack, this is a capability substrate layer problem. The layer receives tasks, residuals, benchmarks, specialist capabilities, resource budgets, representation constraints, and mathematical contracts and must turn them into routes, compressed artifacts, specialist modules, recurrence contracts, benchmark frontiers, and residual records. The hard part is not naming the capability; it is making the boundary explicit enough that a later layer can test, replay, reject, or replace it without guessing what authority was assumed.

It inherits pressure from the previous chapter, Simulation Fidelity and Physical Constraints, and makes that pressure operational in a narrower boundary. It hands its output to Circle Calculus and Proof-Carrying AI Contracts, where the next layer tightens or implements the same constraint. That is why the chapter is written as part of a single stack rather than as a standalone paper summary.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Novel substrates can become authority theater if they are not tied to baselines, adoption gates, and falsification criteria.

In this chapter, the failure is more specific than bad prompting or weak model choice: novel substrates can become authority theater if they are not tied to baselines, adoption gates, and falsification criteria. If `mathematical-and-search-substrates` is left implicit, downstream layers inherit a capability whose authority, evidence burden, and rollback conditions are unclear. The stack therefore treats the missing contract as the defect, not merely the local model behavior.

This does not mean that every layer must be heavyweight. The stack is efficient precisely because small, typed artifacts can carry constraints forward while large models, tools, humans, or specialist modules are invoked only where the current boundary demands them.

## Core Claim

[mathematical-and-search-substrates.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Coils, calculi, and geometric search belong in the stack as optional specialist substrates until tests show where they improve search, routing, or compression.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - GenesisCode, Temporal Coil Research, Cognitive Compilation, TreeLLM, and Simulation Scaling Law - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism turns the core claim into a working contract. First, frame each substrate as a specialist core or IR option. Second, define expected advantage, baseline, and falsification condition. Third, route experiments through evidence gates before adoption.

- Frame each substrate as a specialist core or IR option.
- Define expected advantage, baseline, and falsification condition.
- Route experiments through evidence gates before adoption.

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader or executor should be able to locate the relevant inputs, constraints, residuals, authority grants, and failure conditions. That artifact discipline is what lets the stack improve without making the improvement path opaque.

For implementation, the mechanism should be kept small at first: a schema, a ledger entry, a route decision, a context packet, a proof obligation, or a replayable job record. Only after that minimal form is testable should the layer absorb richer heuristics, learned routing, larger memory, or autonomous repair.

## Interfaces

The interface for Mathematical and Search Substrates is the contract that lets adjacent layers compose without absorbing each other's authority. It should make the chapter's output legible to planning, memory, reasoning, execution, evidence, and improvement layers while preserving the boundary described by the core claim.

- Routing treats substrate as specialist.
- Compression tests representation efficiency.
- Evidence compares against baselines.

The upstream interface accepts the part-level inputs: tasks, residuals, benchmarks, specialist capabilities, resource budgets, representation constraints, and mathematical contracts. The downstream interface emits routes, compressed artifacts, specialist modules, recurrence contracts, benchmark frontiers, and residual records. When either side is ambiguous, the layer should fail closed by producing a missing-contract, missing-evidence, or missing-authority record instead of silently continuing.

This interface also makes chapter insertion and reordering safer. A new chapter can be added when it introduces a genuinely new contract, invariant, or artifact type; otherwise it should merge into the layer that already owns the boundary.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- Exploratory claims stay exploratory.
- Baselines are recorded.
- Failed hypotheses remain visible.

If an invariant cannot be checked directly, the living-book version of the stack should at least record a proxy check and a residual. A weak proxy is acceptable in v0.2 when it is visible; it becomes dangerous only when the manuscript treats it as a completed proof.

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Performance overclaiming.
- Opaque math treated as proof.
- Adoption without regression tests.

For Mathematical and Search Substrates, mitigation starts by turning each listed failure into a named reviewable artifact. When `Performance overclaiming` appears, the system should emit a residual, rejection, quarantine, regression, or human-review record rather than allowing the failure to disappear into later fluent output.

## Minimal implementation

A technical-substrate appendix plan with experiment matrix and adoption gates.

The smallest useful implementation is deliberately narrow: A technical-substrate appendix plan with experiment matrix and adoption gates. It should be easy to validate, easy to render in the book, and easy for a later coding run to convert into a schema, Lean predicate, deterministic fixture, trace table, or replayable command.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Baseline comparison test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Mathematical and Search Substrates boundary. | planned; not run |
| Representation efficiency test | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Mathematical and Search Substrates boundary. | planned; not run |
| Falsification review | Probe whether the chapter claim holds under the relevant Mathematical and Search Substrates boundary. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:substrates.search.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.SearchSubstrates` | A substrate adoption record includes baseline, measured target, and falsification criterion. | planned |
| `lean:substrates.search.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.SearchSubstrates` | A substrate without passing evidence remains non-core. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
|  `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | local raw cache available |
|  `temporal_coil_research` | Temporal Coil Research | mathematical_search_substrate | Manifest-driven A/B process for temporal-coil integration in language model training. | local raw cache available |
|  `cognitive_compilation` | Cognitive Compilation | planning_semantic_ir | Compiler framing for LLM-centered planning, semantic IR, target compilation, incremental repair. | local raw cache available |
|  `treellm` | TreeLLM | semantic_representation | Hierarchical semantic token system for grounded, efficient, explainable language modeling. | local raw cache available |
|  `simulation_scaling` | Simulation Scaling Law | compute_fidelity_constraints | Resource constraints on scope, clockspeed, and fidelity in simulations. | local raw cache available |
|  `coilmoecot` | CoilMoECOT Whitepaper v2.0 | mathematical_search_substrate | Prime-temporal coil networks as governed specialist cores in MoECOT runtime. | source note available; local raw cache available |
|  `circle_calculus_core` | Circle Calculus | proof_carrying_mathematical_substrate | Proof-carrying finite cyclic mathematics project with Lean proofs, Python reference models, Rust utilities, theorem manifests, papers, and Quarto living book. | source note pending |
|  `circle_ai_architectures` | Circle AI Architectures | cyclic_ai_architecture | Disciplined Circle AI thesis: use phase, recurrence, rotation, sparse cyclic mixing, circular memory, harmonic transforms, or geometry-aware structure only where the structure is real and baselines support it. | source note pending |
|  `proof_carrying_circular_computation` | Proof-Carrying Circular Computation | proof_carrying_compute_substrate | CoilIR-style path from circle/coil expressions to dictionary-recognized cyclic structure, Lean-proved rewrite/address transformations, backend selection, and benchmark validation. | source note pending |
|  `theseus_circle_transfer` | Theseus Circle Calculus Transfer Lane | proof_contract_transfer | Report-only bridge from Circle finite fixtures into private Theseus benchmark design with explicit quality/runtime/memory/transfer/failure-case claim boundaries. | source note pending |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Mathematical and Search Substrates defines one boundary in the ASI Stack: Coils, calculi, and geometric search belong in the stack as optional specialist substrates until tests show where they improve search, routing, or compression. Its job is to make a capability more governable, not merely more impressive.

In v0.2, this chapter gives Mathematical and Search Substrates a complete architectural boundary: the source corpus contains exploratory mathematical/search substrates that may matter but must not be overclaimed. Its next maturity step is to substantiate the boundary through `Routing treats substrate as specialist`, preserve `Exploratory claims stay exploratory`, and replace planned proof/test hooks with validated artifacts. Claims should move only when source notes, code, proofs, or run records justify the movement.
